# Downscaling Investigation: Initial Findings

**Study domain:** 24–38°N, 30–38°E (sub-region of the Eastern Mediterranean & Middle East) **Study period:** 1990–1999   
**Notebook:** `notebooks/downscaling_data_exploration.ipynb`   
[`link to github`](https://github.com/Shirlevran9/downscaling-thesis/blob/main/notebooks/downscaling_data_exploration.ipynb)  
---

## **1\. Overview**

The goal of this preliminary project is to explore the use of machine learning (ML) algorithms for statistical downscaling of near-surface temperature, based on a small region and relatively short period. Statistical downscaling exploits the relationship between a coarse predictor of temperature and  fine-resolution temperature fields that are essential for regional climate impact studies that require fine spatial detail. Specifically, our goal in this “pilot” study is to  learn a mapping from a coarse-resolution global  climate model (GCM) temperature output (\~1° grid) to a fine-resolution temperature grid. For the coarse resolution we use CMIP6 data and for the fine resolution the ERA5-Land reanalysis data (0.1° grid). The study region includes a  partial region of the Eastern Mediterranean and Middle East (latitude 24–38°N, longitude 30–38°E, and the study period includes the years1990–1999.  
---

## **2\. Datasets**

| Dataset | Variable | Period | Resolution | Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **ERA5-Land** (target) | 2 m temperature (`t2m`) | 1990–1999 | 0.1° × 0.1° (\~11 km) | Land pixels only; Gregorian calendar, Kelvin |
| **CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM** (predictor) | Near-surface air temperature (`tas`) | 1990–1999 | \~0.94° × 1.25° (\~100 km) | Land and sea pixels; No-leap calendar (29 Feb excluded); Kelvin |

Main datasets dimensions and variables:

| Dataset & Variable | Dimensions	 | Latitude range | Longitude range | Time steps |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| ERA5-Land T2M | time: 3652, latitude: 141, longitude: 81 | 24.00° – 38.00°N | 30.00° – 38.00°E | 3652 |
| CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM TAS | time: 3650, latitude: 17,  longitude: 9, nbnd: 2 | 23.09° – 38.17°N | 28.75° – 38.75°E | 3650 |

**Please explain the lat/lon range for CMIPs; also, do we want padding around the selected region to enable spatial autoregressive models for the full region? part of the Methods section**   
---

## **3\. Data Alignment & Preprocessing**

This section delineates the computational framework established for data alignment and multi-scale preprocessing. Ensuring consistency between the disparate temporal calendars and spatial grids of the ERA5-Land and CMIP6 datasets is a critical prerequisite for subsequent exploratory data analysis, feature engineering, and predictive modeling.  
*Calendar differences:* ERA5-Land uses a Gregorian calendar (including leap days); CMIP6 uses a no-leap calendar. The two leap days in ERA5-Land (1992-02-29 and 1996-02-29) were removed, yielding a shared **3,650-day** period: 1 January 1990 – 31 December 1999\.

*Temperature unit conversion:* Both datasets store temperature in Kelvin (K). Values were converted to degrees Celsius (°C) by subtracting 273.15 from the Kelvin values.  
*Land–sea mask and missing values:* ERA5-Land provides data exclusively over land grid cells; oceanic pixels are assigned NaN. Approximately **32.7%** of grid cells in the pilot target domain contain missing values. This is a structural property of the dataset, not a data quality issue — the same pixels are missing every day in the record, consistent with a static land–sea mask. The missing pattern aligns precisely with the Eastern Mediterranean Sea and the Red Sea. *( Figure.1)*

***Figure.1** A map of the study area showing available ERA5-Land data. Green: valid temperature values over land. Blue: NaN values over sea.  The static land–sea boundary follows the Eastern Mediterranean Sea and the Red Sea. Data: ERA5-Land T2M, 24–38°N, 30–38°E, 1990-1999*.

---

## **4\. Exploratory & Descriptive Analysis**

### **4.1 Main Statistics**

The distribution of the target T2M and predictor TAS variables are summarized in Table 1 and Figure 2\. As evidenced by both the statistical summary and the daily distributions, the **CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM TAS** predictor has an average positive bias. **Ronit: but shouldn’t we point out that max(tas)\<max(t2m)? This is very important. maybe worth checking the 99 percentile or other high percentiles to see where the relation flips?** 

**Table 1**. Descriptive statistics for the target **ERA5-Land T2M** and predictor **CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM TAS** variables 

| Metric | ERA5-Land  T2M (℃)  | CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM  TAS (℃) | Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Minimum** | \-21.3 | \-14.7 |  |
| **25th percentile** | 12.7 | 15.0 |  |
| **Median (50th percentile)** | 20.1 | 21.2 |  |
| **Mean** | 19.2 | 20.8 |  |
| **75th Percentile** | 26.3 | 27.0 |  |
| **90th Percentile** | 29.7 | 31.1 |  |
| **Maximum** | 40.6 | 41.5 |  |
| **Standard Deviation** | 8.7 | 7.9 |  |

**Ronit: ok, but suppose we remove the bias of 1.6 degrees C, well have a smaller max; in other worde, the difference for the max is smaller than for the mean, while we would expect the opposite- that the max of a higher skewed density will move further away**  
![][image1]  
***Figure 2**. Distribution of T2M  (0.1° spatial resolution) and TAS (\~1°) in the region 24–38°N, 30–38°E, 1990-1999. (a) Probability density estimates for  T2M   (blue, land pixels only) and TAS  (orange, all domain cells) and (b) Boxplots showing the distribution across all pixels and all days  Box edges: 25th and 75th percentiles; whiskers: 1.5 × IQR; points: outliers. Ronit: very nice*

### **4.2 Trends Analysis**

This subsection provides a comparative analysis of the sources of temporal variability across both datasets, further corroborating the **systematic warm bias** identified in the **CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM** predictor. The results indicate that both trends remain closely aligned, demonstrating a **near-perfect linear correlation**.

#### **4.2.1 Comparative Temporal Analysis**

Figure 3 encompasses \[maybe shows?\] the temporal variability of theT2M and TAS series in terms of long term trends (annual averages), seasonality (monthly averages) and small scale variability (daily averages), all averaged over the study region. Overall, both variables reproduce consistent long-term trends and seasonal cycles, **\[Ronit: here add a few words on each panel \- from trend through seasonality. try to highlight important points (for example highlight differential conditions under which t2m and tas tend to agree and others where they agree less. Try to  avoid general observations. Also delete from figure caption discussion of the results and move them here, if interesting.  Notably, the absence of a significant warming trend in either dataset contradicts foundational assumptions of this analysis, an observation that will be addressed later on** 

  *Figures* corroborates the **near-perfect linear correlation** between the **ERA5-Land** and **CMIP6** records. This assessment   
![][image2]

***Figure 3a.** Domain-averaged daily 2 m temperature, 1990–1999. ERA5-Land (blue) and CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM (orange) are shown for all 3,650 days. Both datasets exhibit the same unimodal seasonal cycle; CMIP6 is systematically \~1–2°C warmer. Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E.*

*![][image3]*

***Figure 3b. (monthly trend).** Monthly domain-mean 2 m temperature, 1990–1999. Each point is the area-averaged temperature for one calendar month (120 points total, Jan 1990 → Dec 1999), revealing year-to-year variability within each season. The warm bias of CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM (\~1–2°C) persists throughout the decade.* Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E.

*![][image4]*

### ***Figure 3c. (annual trend)*** Annual mean domain-averaged 2 m temperature, 1990–1999. Each point represents the area-averaged temperature for one calendar year, giving 10 data points per dataset. The CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM model is consistently warmer than ERA5-Land by approximately 1–2°C in every year of the record. Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E.

![][image5]

***Figure 3d.** Monthly domain-mean 2 m temperature climatology, 1990–1999. Each point represents the multi-year area-averaged temperature for a specific calendar month. The results corroborate the **systematic warm bias** identified in the **CMIP6** predictor, which remains consistent across the entire seasonal cycle. Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E.*

#### **4.2.2 Warming Trends Analysis**

The foundational framework for this investigation was established in Ronit’s record, `Downscaling using machine learning: Building the database`, *Yosef et al. (2019) showed that*  a significant warming trend was identified in Israel \[add when \- this trend is for a longer period\]:  \-"the warming trends, which are non-monotonic, seem to have been particularly strong since the early 1990s," suggesting that such signals should be detectable in our records. However, as noted, the findings remain inconclusive; this section delineates the evaluative checks performed to identify these potential temporal trends.  
As previously illustrated in the comparative temporal analyses of daily, monthly, and annual means, the anticipated **warming trend** for the 1990–1999 period remains inconclusive. Given that the research conducted by *Yosef et al. (2019)* identified significant signals through the evaluation of daily extrema, this assessment was extended to examine minimum and maximum temperature values across the entire study domain for a specific annual timestamp (arbitrarily selected as August 1st) to determine if a more discernable trend emerges at this level of granularity.  
As evidenced in ***Figure 4a***, the warming signal was not detectable using this specific evaluative mechanism.

![][image6]

***Figure 4a.** Domain-wide min and max temperature on 1 August, 1990–1999. Each year contributes one point per line. With the seasonal cycle removed, upward or downward trends in the 10 data points would indicate a year-over-year warming or cooling signal. The shaded band spans the domain min–max range on that date.  Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E*

Following the inconclusive results of the initial broad assessment, and in alignment with the station-based methodology employed by *Yosef et al. (2019)* across 36 monitoring sites, a refined evaluative approach was adopted. This involved a targeted analysis of a specific temporal slice (August 1st) at a strategically selected grid cell proximal to central Israel. Specifically, coordinates **32°N, 35°E** in the **ERA5-Land** record were matched with **31.57°N, 35°E** in the **CMIP6** dataset. As delineated in *(Figure 4b)*, this granular focus allowed for the detection of a subtle warming signal, with the **ERA5-Land** pixel exhibiting a linear trend of **\+0.217 °C/year** for the August 1st timestamp.  
The **CMIP6** cell demonstrated a linear trend of **\+0.121 °C/year** for the same August 1st period.

### **![][image7]**

***Figure 4a** Temperature at 35°E, 32°N on 1 August, 1990–1999. Each of the 10 points is the value for a single ERA5-Land pixel or CMIP6 cell on that exact date. Dotted lines show the OLS linear trend; the printed slope (°C/year) quantifies the direction and magnitude. Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E*

In alignment with the refined evaluative approach, this assessment examined the commencement of each annual quarter to determine if temperature signals exhibited temporal consistency *(Figure 4c)*; \] \[Ronit: the last sentence is very general and not sure it is accurate. Also try to “lower” the language, what is a signal? To me, it seems that the main take from Figure 4c is that when the trends are inspected by season, the TAS trends are no longer uniformly higher than those of T2M; further, season and year interact, eg. 1994 January 1st and April 1st \] consistent with prior observations, the results remained inconclusive, though more discernible signals were identified for the **January 1st** and **April 1st** timestamps.

![][image8]   
***Figure 4c**. Quarterly mean 2 m temperature at the location nearest to 35°E, 32°N, 1990–1999. Each panel shows one meteorological season (DJF, MAM, JJA, SON). ERA5-Land (blue) and CMIP6 (orange) quarterly means are plotted for each year of the study period. Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E*

---

## **5\. Pairing Process**

As the primary objective of this research is the prediction of fine-resolution temperature fields from coarse-scale model outputs to combine the coarse- and fine-resolution data the smaller pixel should be nested/embedded with the larged pixels, In this initial investigative phase, we  used a simple pairing algorithm to link the two grids. 

### **5.1 Pairing Mechanism**

Each of the **7,683 ERA5-Land** pixels was mapped to the proximal **CMIP6** grid cell utilizing **nearest-neighbour matching** across independent latitude and longitude axes—a geodetic distance approximation was sufficient for these spatial scales. This computational procedure resulted in mapping of the 7,683 fine-pixels (land only) data into **97 unique CMIP6 cells**  \[Ronit: Is that what you mean?\] with the mean number of fine cells per large cell 97 (SD=) and the range (1, 130\) (Table…). This high variability in the number of fine-cells per a large cell is mainly due to shoreline and boundary areas.(Figure 7\) and may substantially influence the resultant analytical outputs  
Also note that the nearest neighbor **method** assigns each fine-resolution pixel to exactly one and only one coarse cell. This means that a fine-cell may have partial spatial overlap with contiguous coarse cells. **\[Ronit: Show a schematic drawing that will make this issue clearer?\]**

| Statistic | Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| Number of CMIP6 cells | 97 |
| Total land pixels assigned | 7,683 |
| Mean number of pixels per cell | \~79.2 |
| Median number of pixels per cell | 97 |
| Std. dev. | \~37.58 |
| Minimum | 1 |
| Maximum | 130 |

***Table 3\.** Statistical attributes characterizing the performance of the nearest-neighbour pairing mechanism established above.*

![][image11]

***Figure 7\.** Number of ERA5-Land (0.1°) land pixels assigned to each CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM grid cell by nearest-neighbour matching. Data: ERA5-Land  in the study region 24–38°N, 30–38°E.*

### 

Several  limitations of the **nearest-neighbor matching** mechanism should be mentioned. First,   
the mechanism necessitates computational padding, as the proximal grid points for boundary-adjacent pixels may reside outside the established study domain. This requirement remains a structural property of any model constrained to a specific sub-region.  
Secondly, the inherent precision of a single "closest" neighbor assignment may not optimally represent local variability; however, after consultative review, this discrepancy was deemed statistically insignificant and was omitted from the current analytical scope.  
Furthermore, the pronounced variance in pixel assignment yields an **unbalanced distribution** across the coarse grid. This lack of uniformity is a persistent issue that may substantially influence the predictive performance of both baseline and high-complexity models

## ---

## **6\. Simple OLS Model**

Subsequent to the implementation of the aforementioned pairing algorithm, a **simple OLS model** was established to further interrogate and characterize the fundamental statistical correspondence between the datasets. This evaluative framework was selected in light of the robust **linear correlation** identified between the **ERA5-Land** and **CMIP6** temperature variables, as delineated in the preceding analysis.

### **6.1 OLS Model Findings**

The bivariate relationship between the coarse-scale CMIP6 TAS and fine-scale ERA5-Land T2M variables serves as the fundamental diagnostic for evaluating downscaling feasibility (Figure 7). The primary findings are as follows:

1. **Near-perfect linear correlation (Pearson *r* ≈ 0.9, Figure 7a).** Aggregated evaluations at the cell level demonstrate that both datasets quantify the same physical variable across disparate spatial scales; consequently, the coarse-grid CMIP6 mean functions as a robust predictor for fine-scale ERA5-Land temperatures. Conversely, correlation at the pixel level exhibits a marginal reduction, as the ERA5-Land record resolves sub-grid variability that remains beyond the resolution of the CMIP6 model.

2. **Systematic intercept bias.** The OLS regression analysis identifies a non-zero intercept, indicating a persistent offset between the datasets. This constant discrepancy is likely attributable to divergent land-surface schemes, varying orographical representations, and the inherent effects of spatial averaging.

3. **Residual spread (downscaling signal).** The variance observed around the regression line characterizes sub-grid spatial heterogeneity—reflecting  influences  such as  localized elevation, land cover, and coastal proximity—which are effectively averaged out in the CMIP6 output but resolved within the ERA5-Land dataset. This residual variability constitutes the primary target signal for the proposed downscaling model.

![][image12]

***Figure 7a.** CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM TAS vs ERA5-Land T2M in the study region 24–38°N, 30–38°E. 1990–1999. (a): hexbin density plot of all pixel×day pairs; colour encodes log-density. Panel (b): cell-level daily means with OLS regression line (Pearson r annotated). TAS,.*

### **6.2 OLS Model Residuals Diagnostics**

As previously established in the regression analysis *(Figure 7a)*, the residuals exhibit a largely uniform distribution around the regression line. This statistical consistency raises critical questions regarding the underlying drivers of the variance between the fine-resolution **ERA5-Land T2M** targets and the coarse-scale **CMIP6 TAS** predictors. This section delineates the analytical framework established to interrogate these discrepancies.  
***Note: This section constitutes a work-in-progress; further investigative analyses are currently being conducted.***

#### **6.2.1 Distance from center**

The initial investigative hypothesis posited that **residual magnitude** would exhibit a positive correlation with the spatial distance of the **ERA5-Land** pixel from the **CMIP6** cell centroid; however, the empirical results delineated in *(Figure 7b1)* effectively disprove this assumption.  
As evidenced by the diagnostic plots, the **residual magnitude** demonstrates a substantially uniform distribution across the spectrum of spatial distances, with only a marginal increase discernible within the 0.3–0.5° range.  
![][image13]

***Figure 7b1.** OLS residual diagnostics, 1990–1999. Panel (a): residuals versus CMIP6 TAS value; a horizontal band near zero indicates homoscedasticity. Panel (b): residuals versus Euclidean distance from the assigned CMIP6 cell centre in degrees; a non-zero slope would indicate boundary-assignment artefacts. Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E Ronit: no, not this one. The previous one denoted here as 7a*

#### **6.2.2 Residuals by Sea Percentage of CMIP Cells**

Given that the **CMIP6** domain encompasses maritime regions ( missing T2M values) it was hypothesized that the **residual magnitude** might be influenced by the maritime fraction of a given predictor cell. The maritime percentage was quantified by approximating the total number of fine-resolution grid cells contained within each **CMIP6** cell and determining the proportion of corresponding null values. As illustrated in *(Figure 7b2)*, the correlation between maritime percentage and the OLS residuals revealed no significant evidence of a systemic increase or decrease in **residual magnitude** along this parameter.

***![][image14]***

***Figure 7b2.** OLS residual distribution by CMIP6 cell sea fraction, 1990–1999. Each box summarises all pixel×day residuals for CMIP6 cells by the fraction of missing T2M (sea fraction)(divided into 20% intervals (bins).**Ronit: Add boxplot definitions \- each figure should be self explanatory** Ronit: as above, define the box limits, whiskers etc. Its not enough to define it once… Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E*.

#### **6.2.3 Residuals by the number  of Paired ERA5 Cells**

In alignment with the preceding analyses, the subsequent hypothesis posited that the **residual magnitude** would demonstrate a correlation with the quantity of **ERA5-Land** pixels assigned to a specific **CMIP6** cell. Given the structural relationship between these variables, the data was stratified into discrete bins; however, the empirical results remained inconsistent, leading to the conclusion that the number of paired cells is not a primary driver of the observed variance *(Figure 7b3)*.  
![][image15]

**Figure 7b3.** OLS residual distribution by CMIP6 cell land-pixel count, 1990–1999. Each box summarises all pixel×day residuals for CMIP6 cells within a given pixel-count quantile bin. Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E.

#### **6.2.4 Residuals by Time Period**

Following the assessment of annual cycles and their potential influence on the statistical correspondence between the datasets, an evaluation was conducted to examine **residual magnitude** in relation to the seasonal cycle, specifically across monthly and quarterly timestamps *(Figures 7b4 and 7b5)*. A robust statistical relationship was identified, with the **March–May quarter** (particularly April and May) demonstrating the most pronounced **residual distribution**, whereas the summer months exhibited the smallest variance. This observation is consistent with regional climatological dynamics, where the stable, elevated temperatures of months such as August yield higher consistency, while the spring season is characterized by greater spatio-temporal variability.

![][image16]  
***Figure 7b4.** OLS residual distribution by meteorological season, 1990–1999. Each box summarises all pixel×day residuals (ERA5-Land T2M minus OLS-predicted T2M) for that season across the full study domain. Outliers are suppressed for clarity. A median consistently above or below zero indicates a seasonal bias in the global OLS fit. Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E.*  
**![][image17]**  
***Figure 7b5.** OLS residual distribution by calendar month, 1990–1999. Each box summarises all pixel × day residuals for that month across the full study domain (Jan 1990 – Dec 1999, \~300 daily snapshots per month × 7,683 land pixels). Outliers are suppressed for clarity. A systematic pattern across months—for example, a cold bias in winter months and a warm bias in summer—would indicate that the global OLS slope is insufficient and that season-specific or non-linear models are required. Data: ERA5-Land T2M and CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM TAS, 24–38°N, 30–38°E.*.

### ---

### **8\. Feature Engineering (work in progress)**

***Note \- This part is WIP and there's a lot to add here still***

We define the area-weighted global mean daily TAS computed as the average TAS over all grid cells worldwide. This feature provides a large-scale thermodynamic predictor. This global signal encodes the overall state of the climate system on each day and may improve downscaling performance beyond the local cell temperature alone by capturing the background warming state.  
Figure 8, shows the global mean TAS time series for 1990–1999. The seasonal cycle is clearly visible, and the global mean is substantially lower (\~14°C) than the EMME regional mean (\~20.5°C), as expected for a global average that includes polar regions.  
![][image18]  
***Figure 8\.** Area-weighted global mean daily near-surface air temperature (TAS) from CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM, 1990–1999. The seasonal cycle reflects the Northern Hemisphere’s dominance of land area. Data: CMIP6 CESM2-WACCM, global domain.*

### **9\. Feature Engineering \- Space & Time Correlation**

***Note \- This part is WIP it exists only in the code***

This section interrogates the **statistical correspondence** between disparate **CMIP6** grid cells and a specific **ERA5-Land** target cell across both spatial and temporal dimensions.
